one_line_takeaway: By exploiting the memory-write and memory-retrieval mechanics of self-evolving LLM agents (recursive self-replication for sliding-window FIFO buffers, semantic aliasing for RAG retrieval), a single black-box exposure to poisoned web content converts transient indirect prompt injection into persistent, cross-session agent compromise that survives truncation/filtering and evades prompt-level defenses (ASR >60% even under guardrails).
key_finding: The Zombie Agent significantly outperforms all four IPI baselines on cumulative ASR for both Data Exfiltration and Command Execution, in both Sliding-Window and RAG settings (Figure 3), with baseline ASR decaying toward zero after the context/window fills while the proposed attack sustains high ASR. For Sliding Window, the attack achieves 100% payload retention across 20+ trigger rounds via recursive renewal, whereas baseline payloads' retention rate drops to zero once the FIFO buffer flushes them (Figure 5a). For RAG, the attack accumulates roughly 2.5x more payload copies in the vector database than baselines (~240 vs ~100 copies) and retrieves roughly 23 malicious entries at K=50 in the Top-K context, versus far lower baseline retrieval density (Figure 5b/5c). Across three memory-evolution/consolidation strategies, Raw History storage yields the highest ASR (~77%), while more filtering-oriented strategies -- Verbal Reflection (~12% ASR) and Refined Experience (~3-15% ASR) -- reduce but do not eliminate the attack; the authors note that even the reduced ~15% Command Execution rate under filtering represents the agent 'crystallizing' the payload into a permanent rule (Figure 4). Against three instruction-level prompt defenses (Sandwich, Instructional, Spotlighting), ASR remains above 60% across all settings, only 10-15% lower than with no defense (Figure 6), because these defenses only guard the immediate context window and do not address the memory-consolidation pathway. Two qualitative case studies (a healthcare EHR-summarization agent and an e-commerce shopping agent) illustrate real-world consequences: exfiltration of a patient's diagnosis and SSN via a hidden URL parameter, and fraudulent-vendor purchase redirection plus exfiltration of a user's home address/phone number.
core_contribution: The paper formalizes and demonstrates a persistent, cross-session attack against self-evolving LLM agents (agents that write and reuse long-term memory across sessions), which the authors name the 'Zombie Agent' attack. Unlike ordinary indirect prompt injection (transient, discarded when a session ends), the attack has two phases: (1) Infection -- a black-box attacker publishes a payload in external content (e.g., a webpage) that the agent reads during a benign task, and the agent's own memory-update function FM commits the payload into long-term memory; (2) Trigger -- in a later, unrelated session, the payload is retrieved from memory and causes unauthorized tool actions (e.g., data exfiltration, command execution) while the agent continues to appear useful on benign tasks. The authors design mechanism-specific persistence techniques for the two dominant memory architectures: for Sliding-Window (FIFO) memory, a 'Recursive Self-Replication' payload that instructs the agent to rewrite/revisit the payload on every turn so it survives truncation; for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) memory, 'Semantic Aliasing' -- wrapping the malicious instruction in diverse carrier sentences that map to broad, high-frequency embedding clusters so it is retrieved for semantically unrelated future queries.
threat_model: Strict black-box attacker with no access to model parameters (theta), no access to the agent's memory state (Mj) or private user history, and no ability to modify the agent's toolset. The attacker's only capability is publishing malicious content on external sources (e.g., public webpages) that the agent may read during a benign session, so the content enters the session transcript Cj and can influence the memory-evolution function FM. The attacker's goal is to get a payload Z written into memory (Z in Mj+1) and later trigger unauthorized behavior (covert data exfiltration or attacker-chosen tool actions) in a later session Ck (k>j), while preserving normal task utility so the compromise stays covert.
limitations: Explicitly acknowledged by the authors: the paper only evaluates two specific commercial LLMs (Gemini-2.5-Flash and GLM-4.7-Flash) and two canonical memory architectures (Sliding Window, RAG); other self-evolving memory designs (e.g., MemGPT-style tiered memory) are not evaluated empirically, only discussed in Related Work. The evaluation of instruction-level defenses covers only prompt-based guardrails (Sandwich, Instructional, Spotlighting, plus an 'Instruction Guard' filter) and does not test other categories of defense (e.g., provenance tracking, policy checks on tool calls) that the authors themselves propose as future mitigations in the Conclusion. The healthcare and e-commerce scenarios are described as qualitative case studies, not systematically measured across many trials the way the ASR/persistence experiments are. The authors explicitly flag as future work: evaluating defenses under adaptive attackers, and clarifying which agent designs/tool-permission configurations make persistent compromise most likely.

datasets_used: data-for-agents/insta-150k-v1 (real-world web-interaction queries used as Phase-II 'irrelevant benign task' pool)
quantitative_results: Sliding Window: Zombie Agent payload retention = 100% across 20+ trigger rounds, vs. baseline IPI retention decaying to 0% after context window truncation (Figure 5a)
RAG: Zombie Agent accumulates ~240 payload copies in the memory database vs. ~100 for baselines (~2.5x more) (Figure 5b)
RAG: Zombie Agent retrieves ~23 malicious entries in Top-K context at K=50, exceeding baseline retrieval density (Figure 5c)
ASR by evolution strategy: Raw History ~77% ASR, Verbal Reflection ~12% ASR, Refined Experience ~3-15% ASR (Figure 4)
Defense evasion: ASR remains >60% across all three tested instruction-level defenses (Sandwich, Instructional, Spotlighting), a drop of only ~10-15% versus no defense (Figure 6)

Experimental protocol scale: Sliding Window K=3 exposure rounds, M=20 trigger rounds; RAG K=300 exposure rounds against a 3,000-entry database, M=20 trigger rounds; all results averaged over 3 random-seed runs
baselines_compared: IPI Naive (plain-text malicious command in poisoned webpage)
IPI Context Ignoring ('ignore previous instructions' pattern)
IPI Escape Characters (syntactic delimiters to break data encapsulation)
IPI Fake Completion (mimics agent's chat template to fake a system instruction)
